evidence: crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs imports and mutates temper_server internals (`ServerEventStore`, `SpecRegistry`, `VerificationStatus`) instead of depending on a boundary trait (lines 9-12, 212-294, 297-369).

crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs reaches directly into persistence (`state.persistent_store()`) and reshapes store rows into API JSON in handlers (lines 24-49, 89-107), coupling API surface to storage row format.

crates/temper-server/src/insight_generator.rs contains multiple responsibilities in one module: insight generation, unmet-intent synthesis, feature-request synthesis, dedup key policy, and error taxonomy (lines 48-402, 414+).

crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs duplicates full tenant/spec restoration orchestration for Postgres and Turso, indicating missing decomposition around backend-independent restore flow (lines 212-294 vs 296-369).
crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs duplicates handler-level shaping/fallback logic across records and insights endpoints (lines 23-80 and 88-127).

`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs` directly imports and orchestrates multiple concrete backends (`temper_store_turso`, `temper_store_redis`, `sqlx`, `ClickHouseStore`) in one command path, creating a heavy dependency surface in a single module.

`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs` and `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs` use Turso-specific reads/writes via `state.persistent_store()` while returning empty/silent fallbacks when unavailable, so behavior depends on a specific backend despite broader storage options in CLI.
`GET /observe/evolution/feature-requests` in `operations.rs` performs generation + DB upsert + entity creation before listing, mixing query and command responsibilities in one read endpoint.

Policy management seams are inconsistent in `api.rs`: approve flow persists policies to Turso, while direct policy CRUD handlers mutate in-memory state only, creating cross-boundary lifecycle divergence with startup hydration in `serve/mod.rs`.
`crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs` uses `#[allow(deprecated)]` on a root re-export (`pub use store::RecordStore;`), preserving an old API path without a bounded migration mechanism.

Several assigned files are pass-through shims with little/no local behavior (e.g., `crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs`, `crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs`, `crates/temper-odata/src/query/mod.rs`), increasing indirection without added semantics.
`crates/temper-observe/src/schema.rs` duplicates near-identical column-membership helpers and corresponding tests for each table, indicating repetitive hand-maintained logic.
holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `crates/temper-server/src/` has `file_count: 22`, exceeding the batch threshold signal for flat-directory overload (>10 files with mixed concerns).
`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs` is 1281 LOC and mixes unrelated endpoint families (secrets, policies, decision workflow, agent authz/audit, REPL) in one root file.

Management router wiring in `api.rs` delegates multiple `/api/*` mutation routes to `crate::observe::*` handlers (`/specs/load-dir`, `/specs/load-inline`, `/wasm/modules/*`, `/evolution/*`), creating placement mismatch between route ownership and folder naming.
`records_list.rs` has no tests for Turso errors, missing-store fallback, or JSON merge behavior in API responses (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:19-128`).

`storage.rs` tests cover URL redaction and status mapping only (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:371-420`), but there are no parity tests asserting Postgres/Turso restore paths produce equivalent registry outcomes across `load_registry_from_postgres` and `load_registry_from_turso` (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:212-369`).

`AuthzEngine::permissive()` creates an empty policy set that denies by default, but its name implies allow-all behavior (`crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs:56`, tests at `crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs:302-303`).
The same misleading semantic appears next to a real allow-all implementation (`PermissiveWasmAuthzGate`) in `crates/temper-server/src/wasm_authz_gate.rs:104-121`, increasing reader confusion.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Misleading security-critical naming (`permissive`) contradicts runtime behavior and adjacent naming in WASM authz.

evidence: Multiple handlers log query failures and then return successful empty payloads, making control flow obscure for clients (e.g. `crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:604-609`, `954-959`; `crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs:110-115`, `166-171`).
This pattern hides whether a response is genuinely empty or backend-unavailable, reducing operational clarity.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Failure and empty-data flows are intentionally merged in several endpoints, obscuring true execution outcomes.

evidence: API query/body fields that are bounded enums are typed as free-form strings and validated manually (`scope: String` in `crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:627`, matched at `644`; `record_type/status: Option<String>` in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:12-14`; `disposition` string normalization in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs:313`).
This repeats stringly-typed protocol parsing instead of leveraging typed deserialization.

`SecurityContext::from_headers` defaults missing/unknown principal kind to `System` (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs:53`), while `authorize_or_bypass` unconditionally allows `System` (`crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs:225-226`).
Server authorization paths call this bypass-enabled flow (`crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs:267`), so missing identity headers can violate expected auth contract.

Read APIs treat backend query failures as `200 OK` with empty collections (`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:604-609`, `954-959`; `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:67-72`; `crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs:110-115`).
Write/authorize paths in the same modules return explicit non-2xx on failures (e.g. `crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:349-358`, `410-417`, `522-527`).

Most environment and config reads occur inside runtime setup/handlers, not at module import time (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs:71-119`, `343-346`; `crates/temper-server/src/webhooks.rs:70-87`, `188`).
Embedded specs via `include_str!` are compile-time constants in bootstrap/test contexts (`crates/temper-platform/src/bootstrap.rs:20-55`) and do not introduce runtime boot-order side effects.
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: Some startup paths still bind behavior to ambient env values (e.g., webhook header expansion) without explicit config provenance.

evidence: Header conventions drift across modules: `extract_temper_headers` accepts any `x-temper-*` keys (`crates/temper-server/src/authz_helpers.rs:16-26`) while `SecurityContext::from_headers` only interprets specific keys (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs:60-85`).

Tests in authz helpers validate `x-temper-principal` (`crates/temper-server/src/authz_helpers.rs:155-173`), but authz context parsing expects `x-temper-principal-id` (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs:62`).

Several sensitive tenant/cross-tenant endpoints are routed without explicit authorization checks (secrets and decision listing/streams in `crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:68-101`, handlers at `163`, `253`, `290`, `561`, `886`, `915`, `975`, `1001`).
Other sibling policy/decision mutation endpoints enforce Cedar checks and denial recording (`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:341-358`, `443-460`, `640-641`, `815-816`).

Missing or malformed identity headers can become `System` and bypass all checks (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs:53`; `crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs:225-226`; invoked from `crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs:267`).

Authorization logic is duplicated per-handler instead of centralized middleware/policy guard, producing drift between guarded and unguarded sibling routes (`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs:341-358`, `443-460`, `640-641`, `815-816` vs unguarded handlers at `163`, `290`, `561`, `915`).
Shared helper exists (`crates/temper-server/src/authz_helpers.rs`) but is not applied consistently to route protection decisions.

findings.summary: CLI and API layers directly depend on persistence and server internals, creating a broad coupling hotspot.
evidence: storage.rs imports `temper_server::event_store::ServerEventStore` and `temper_server::registry::*` and performs registry rehydration directly (lines 9-12, 272-289, 347-364).
records_list.rs handlers directly call `state.persistent_store()` and merge raw `r.data` JSON into response objects (lines 24-49, 89-107), bypassing a domain service boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a storage-agnostic application service trait (e.g., evolution/spec read-model service) in a neutral crate and move row-to-domain/API mapping there; make CLI and HTTP handlers depend on that trait instead of concrete store/server internals.

findings.summary: Postgres and Turso registry restore paths are duplicated instead of sharing one backend-neutral orchestration.
evidence: storage.rs repeats near-identical flow in `load_registry_from_postgres` and `load_registry_from_turso`: fetch rows, group by tenant, parse CSDL, build ioa pairs, register tenant, set verification status (lines 212-294 and 296-369).

records_list.rs shows similar per-endpoint manual row-to-JSON projection/fallback patterns (lines 23-80 and 88-127), reinforcing a broader pattern of backend/shape-specific duplication at module boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared restore orchestration into a generic function over a backend adapter trait (load specs + constraints + row type), and similarly centralize evolution row-to-response projection in a shared mapper.

findings.summary: Insight generator module mixes multiple domain pipelines and policy concerns in one file.
evidence: insight_generator.rs implements at least three separate pipelines (`generate_insights`, `generate_unmet_intents`, `generate_feature_requests`) plus error categorization and dedup policy in one unit (lines 48-402, 414+).

records_list.rs serves ranked insights from persistent records while also flattening JSON payload fields into endpoint output (lines 83-127), making API shape sensitive to broad policy changes in generator output.
findings.suggestion: Split `insight_generator.rs` into focused modules (`insight_pipeline`, `unmet_intent_pipeline`, `feature_request_pipeline`, `error_taxonomy`) and define a typed read-model contract consumed by `records_list` to stabilize ownership and change boundaries.

findings.summary: Feature-request listing endpoint mutates persistent/domain state on every read call
evidence: `handle_feature_requests` (`operations.rs` lines ~219-267) generates feature requests, upserts them, and dispatches `CreateFeatureRequest` actions before returning list data.
`generate_feature_requests` creates new `RecordHeader` IDs (`insight_generator.rs` lines ~387-390), so repeated GET requests produce new IDs and fresh inserts.
`feature_requests.id` is the primary key (`schema.rs` line ~189) and upsert conflict key (`store.rs` lines ~567-571), so changing IDs bypass deduplication.
findings.suggestion: Split this into explicit command/query seams: move generation/upsert/entity-creation to a scheduled job or POST endpoint, and keep GET as pure read from persisted projections keyed by a stable dedup key.

findings.summary: Policy handlers diverge on persistence semantics, causing restart-time split brain
evidence: `handle_put_policies` and `handle_add_policy_rule` (`api.rs` lines ~334-546) only update `state.tenant_policies` in memory; they do not call `upsert_tenant_policy`.
`handle_approve_decision` (`api.rs` lines ~731-742) does persist policies to Turso, creating a different lifecycle for logically similar policy updates.
Startup in `serve/mod.rs` (`lines ~241-269`) hydrates policies from Turso into memory, so in-memory-only updates are lost after restart.
findings.suggestion: Centralize policy mutation behind one service method that always performs validate -> in-memory commit -> durable upsert (or explicit transaction/rollback), then reuse it across approve and CRUD handlers.

findings.summary: Turso-specific behavior leaks into server features despite multi-backend CLI surface
evidence: `serve/mod.rs` supports Postgres/Turso/Redis initialization (`lines ~74-127`), but multiple API/evolution handlers directly assume Turso via `state.persistent_store()`.
When Turso is unavailable, handlers return empty/silent fallbacks (`operations.rs` lines ~297-299, `api.rs` lines ~609-620 and ~959-970) instead of backend-neutral behavior.
This creates capability skew where the configured backend controls endpoint semantics, not just storage implementation.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a backend-neutral persistence trait for decisions/policies/evolution records and route all handlers through that abstraction; enforce explicit `501/503` for unsupported capabilities instead of silent empty responses.

findings.summary: Unused policy concatenation logic adds noise and obscures the real validation path
evidence: In `handle_add_policy_rule`, `let mut all = String::new(); ...` is constructed (`api.rs` lines ~499-505) but never used.
A second `combined` string is built and used for `reload_policies` (`api.rs` lines ~509-523), making the first traversal dead logic.
findings.suggestion: Delete the unused `all` assembly block and keep a single policy-composition path before `reload_policies` to reduce branch noise and future maintenance mistakes.

findings.summary: Simulation loop advances scheduler twice but processes only first delivery set
evidence: `run_simulation_impl` calls `let delivered = sched.tick();` (`simulation.rs` line ~204), processes `delivered`, then calls `sched.tick();` again (`line ~234`) with no handling of second returned messages.
Verification cascade consumers (`verify/mod.rs` and bootstrap verification in `bootstrap.rs`) rely on this simulation path, so dropped/ignored second-tick deliveries can skew verification outcomes.
findings.suggestion: Use a single tick per loop or process deliveries from both ticks explicitly (with clear intended semantics); add a regression test asserting no delivered message batch is ignored.

findings.summary: Deferred/scheduled dispatch paths drop caller context and break seam consistency
evidence: Scheduled follow-up actions use `AgentContext::default()` (`actions.rs` lines ~149-157), discarding original `agent_id/session_id` propagation from the triggering call.
`create_system_entity` also dispatches with `AgentContext::default()` (`operations.rs` lines ~47-50), so provenance differs by path rather than operation intent.
findings.suggestion: Thread an explicit provenance policy through deferred/system dispatch helpers (inherit caller context, or attach a typed system actor identity) and enforce it consistently at dispatch entry points.

findings.summary: Deprecated `RecordStore` is still exported from crate root behind `#[allow(deprecated)]`, extending dual-path API debt.
evidence: `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:27-28` suppresses deprecation and re-exports `store::RecordStore`.
The same root also exports the newer record-centric surface (`records::*` at `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:22-26`), so callers can continue using legacy and new APIs concurrently.
findings.suggestion: Replace blanket suppression with a bounded migration path: either remove root export and force `store::RecordStore` explicit imports, or gate a compatibility re-export behind a feature flag plus removal milestone.

findings.summary: Multiple modules exist only as re-export shims, adding namespace hops without behavior.
evidence: `crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs:3` is only `pub use temper_sandbox::repl::{ReplConfig, run_repl};`.
`crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs:3` is only a naming-function re-export.
`crates/temper-odata/src/query/mod.rs:9-13` is primarily pass-through re-export wiring to `filter`/`types`.
findings.suggestion: Either collapse these shims by importing from canonical modules directly, or annotate them as temporary compatibility facades with deprecation attributes and a planned removal version.

findings.summary: Schema column validation uses repeated table-specific helper/test blocks instead of a shared pattern.
evidence: `crates/temper-observe/src/schema.rs:65-77` defines three near-identical `is_*_column` functions that differ only by backing slice.
Tests at `crates/temper-observe/src/schema.rs:84-122` repeat the same assertion pattern for each table.
These constants/helpers are part of the public crate surface via `crates/temper-observe/src/lib.rs:34`.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared helper (e.g., table enum + generic membership check) and table-driven tests to reduce repetitive maintenance and prevent drift when schemas evolve.

findings.summary: Mutation handlers for `/api` are physically placed under `observe`, violating folder-role boundaries.
evidence: `api.rs:1-5` claims management endpoints are separate from read-only observe routes.
`api.rs:42-65` routes `/api/specs/*`, `/api/wasm/modules/*`, and `/api/evolution/*` to `crate::observe::*` handlers.
`observe/evolution.rs:8-14` publicly re-exports mutation handlers used by `/api` (`handle_decide`, `handle_unmet_intent`, `handle_sentinel_check`) from the observe namespace.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `crates/temper-server/src/api/` with `specs.rs`, `wasm.rs`, `evolution.rs`; (2) move `/api` mutation handlers out of `observe/*` into those files, keeping only read-side `/observe` endpoints in `observe/`; (3) update router imports in `api.rs` and `observe/mod.rs`; (4) run `rg "crate::observe::(specs|wasm|evolution)::handle" crates/temper-server/src`, then `cargo fmt` and `cargo test -p temper-server` to validate no stale imports.

findings.summary: Root `temper-server/src` is overloaded and `api.rs` concentrates multiple unrelated domains.
evidence: Directory profile reports `crates/temper-server/src/` has 22 production files while adjacent concerns already use subfolders (`observe/`, `state/`, `reaction/`, `entity_actor/`).
`api.rs` is 1281 lines and contains separate phases/features (secrets, policy CRUD, decision workflow, agent authz/audit, REPL), indicating mixed ownership in one root module.
`authz_helpers.rs` and `constraint_engine.rs` remain as root-level cross-cutting modules despite nearby domain folders where their concerns are consumed.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) split `api.rs` into `api/secrets.rs`, `api/policies.rs`, `api/decisions.rs`, `api/agent.rs`, `api/repl.rs`; (2) introduce `mod api;` submodule routing assembly in a slim root `api.rs`; (3) move `authz_helpers.rs` to `security/authz_helpers.rs` and `constraint_engine.rs` to `state/constraints.rs` (or `invariants/constraints.rs`) with transitional re-exports; (4) run `rg "crate::(authz_helpers|constraint_engine)" crates/temper-server/src`, then `cargo fmt` and `cargo test -p temper-server`.

findings.summary: `temper-observe` exposes query-store and telemetry-emission APIs at the same crate root, weakening role clarity.
evidence: `lib.rs:8-11` describes architecture centered on `ObservabilityStore` query interfaces/adapters.
`lib.rs:37` re-exports `emit_metrics`, `emit_span`, and `from_transition` from `wide_event`, introducing producer APIs into the same top-level surface as query/store interfaces.
`clickhouse.rs` is strictly query-adapter code, reinforcing the mismatch between documented query-centric ownership and mixed root exports.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create explicit subdomains `query/` and `emit/` under `temper-observe/src`; (2) move `clickhouse`, `store`, `schema`, `memory` into `query/` and keep `wide_event`/`otel` under `emit/`; (3) change root `lib.rs` to re-export only one curated facade per subdomain (for example `pub mod query; pub mod emit;`), preserving compatibility via temporary deprecated re-exports; (4) run `rg "temper_observe::(emit_|ClickHouseStore|ObservabilityStore)" crates/` and `cargo test -p temper-observe` to validate import updates.

findings.summary: Runtime evolution APIs violate the documented multi-backend storage contract.
evidence: README marks both Postgres and Turso storage as implemented (`README.md:216`).

`list_evolution_records` and `list_evolution_insights` only call `state.persistent_store()` (Turso) and otherwise return empty arrays (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:23-80,88-127`).
CLI/server bootstrap still provisions Postgres evolution storage (`PostgresRecordStore::migrate`) indicating expected non-Turso support (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:49-54`).
findings.suggestion: Define one explicit compatibility policy and enforce it end-to-end: either (1) make evolution read APIs backend-agnostic by introducing a shared `EvolutionReadStore` trait with Postgres+Turso implementations, or (2) explicitly declare Turso-only for evolution and fail fast on non-Turso startup. Update README and route behavior to match that policy (no silent empty fallback).

findings.summary: Postgres and Turso registry restore paths duplicate orchestration logic.
evidence: `load_registry_from_postgres` and `load_registry_from_turso` repeat the same steps (group rows by tenant, parse CSDL, build IOA pairs, register tenant, set verification statuses) across `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:212-369`.

Because evolution reads are already backend-split in server routes (`records_list.rs` Turso-only), this duplicate restore orchestration compounds boundary divergence risk instead of centralizing backend differences.
findings.suggestion: Extract a backend-neutral restore pipeline: create a shared internal function that accepts normalized tenant/spec rows via a small adapter trait, and keep only fetch/parsing differences in backend-specific adapters. Then add a single parity test harness that runs both adapters through identical fixtures.

findings.summary: Critical evolution governance read paths lack contract-level tests.
evidence: `records_list.rs` has no unit/integration tests despite containing response-shaping logic, error fallback behavior, and filtering contract (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:19-128`).

`storage.rs` backend logic has tests only for helper functions (`redact_connection_url`, `row_to_registry_status`) and none for backend parity in load/upsert flows (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:371-420` vs production paths at `212-369`).
`events.rs` tests validate serialization only; no endpoint behavior tests for dropped/lagged broadcast events (`crates/temper-server/src/events.rs`).
findings.suggestion: Add a governance API test matrix: (1) route-level tests for `/observe/evolution/records` and `/observe/evolution/insights` covering success, Turso error, and no-store behavior, (2) backend parity tests for Postgres vs Turso spec restore using shared fixtures, and (3) SSE endpoint tests validating behavior under lagged broadcast/error conditions.

findings.summary: Evolution concerns are split across root and observe folders, reducing navigability.
evidence: Structure profile reports a flat `crates/temper-server/src/` with 22 production files (mixed concerns).

`insight_generator.rs` is evolution-domain logic but sits at root, while adjacent evolution handlers are in `observe/evolution/` and consume it through cross-folder imports (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs:16`).
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 target folders: create `crates/temper-server/src/evolution/` with `insights.rs` (from `insight_generator.rs`) and shared evolution domain types; keep HTTP handlers in `observe/evolution/`. Stage 2 move order: move `insight_generator.rs` first, update `lib.rs` module exports, then update imports in `observe/evolution/operations.rs`. Stage 3 import-update/validation commands: run `rg -n "insight_generator|crate::evolution" crates/temper-server/src`, `cargo check -p temper-server`, and `cargo test -p temper-server` to verify no stale paths and preserved behavior.

findings.summary: Missing identity headers default to System principal and bypass Cedar checks
evidence: `SecurityContext::from_headers` initializes `kind` as `PrincipalKind::System` (`context.rs:53`).
`AuthzEngine::authorize_or_bypass` allows all `System` principals (`engine.rs:225-226`).
Server authorization paths use `authorize_or_bypass` (`entity_ops.rs:267`), so absent/invalid headers can become allow-all.
findings.suggestion: Change default principal kind for missing/unknown headers to a non-bypass identity (e.g., `Anonymous`/`Customer`/`Agent` with deny-by-default), and reserve `System` only for explicitly trusted internal construction paths (e.g., `SecurityContext::system()`).

findings.summary: Sensitive API routes lack Cedar authorization while sibling routes enforce it
evidence: Secrets and decision read/stream handlers are routed and implemented without auth checks (`api.rs` routes `68-101`; handlers `163`, `253`, `290`, `561`, `886`, `915`, `975`, `1001`).
Policy mutation and decision mutation paths do enforce authorization and denial auditing (`api.rs:341-358`, `443-460`, `640-641`, `815-816`).
findings.suggestion: Apply a uniform auth guard/middleware for all management and decision routes, with explicit allowlist for intentionally public endpoints; use one reusable helper to avoid per-handler divergence.

findings.summary: Backend query failures are returned as empty 200 responses
evidence: Decision list endpoints log query errors but return empty success payloads (`api.rs:604-609`, `954-959`).
Observe agents/evolution endpoints follow the same pattern (`observe/agents.rs:110-115`, `166-171`; `records_list.rs:67-72`, `118-123`).
findings.suggestion: Return explicit backend-failure status (`503`/`500`) with error bodies for query failures, and keep `200 + empty` only for true empty datasets; centralize this mapping in a shared helper.

findings.summary: Enum-like request/query fields are modeled as free-form strings
evidence: `ApproveBody.scope` is `String` and manually matched (`api.rs:627`, `644-652`).
Evolution record filters are `Option<String>` for bounded domains (`records_list.rs:12-14`).
Feature request disposition is normalized with manual lowercase matching (`operations.rs:313-319`).
findings.suggestion: Replace string fields with serde-deserializable enums (`#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]` / aliases where needed) so invalid values fail at parse boundaries consistently.

findings.summary: Header naming conventions drift between extraction and authz parsing
evidence: `extract_temper_headers` forwards any `x-temper-*` key (`authz_helpers.rs:16-26`), but `SecurityContext::from_headers` recognizes only specific names (`context.rs:60-85`).
Helper tests validate `x-temper-principal` (`authz_helpers.rs:155-173`), while parsing logic requires `x-temper-principal-id` (`context.rs:62`).
Dispatch context also accepts different identity headers (`x-agent-id` and fallback `x-temper-principal-id`) (`dispatch.rs:34-54`).
findings.suggestion: Define a single header schema (constants + parser) and enforce it in helpers/tests; reject or explicitly map legacy aliases instead of silently ignoring mismatched keys.

findings.summary: Authorization policy is duplicated at handler level, causing drift
evidence: Some API handlers inline auth checks and denial handling (`api.rs:341-358`, `443-460`, `640-641`, `815-816`) while sibling handlers skip checks entirely (`api.rs:163`, `290`, `561`, `915`; observe handlers in `observe/entities.rs`).
A shared helper exists but is used only for parts of the surface (`authz_helpers.rs`), indicating missing structural abstraction for route-level authorization.
findings.suggestion: Introduce route-level authorization middleware/guard builders that declare required action/resource once per route group; keep denial recording in one shared path.

findings.summary: `permissive` name contradicts deny-by-default behavior
evidence: `AuthzEngine::permissive()` creates `PolicySet::new()` (`engine.rs:56-60`), and tests confirm this denies by default (`engine.rs:302-310`).
In WASM authz, `PermissiveWasmAuthzGate` is true allow-all (`wasm_authz_gate.rs:104-121`), making the `permissive` engine name semantically inverted.
findings.suggestion: Rename `AuthzEngine::permissive` to an accurate term like `empty_policy_set`/`default_deny`, and update call sites/tests to remove security-behavior ambiguity.

findings.summary: Security context contract grants privileged semantics on missing headers
evidence: Context construction from headers treats unknown principal kind as `System` (`context.rs:64-68`).
Auth engine defines `System` as unconditional bypass (`engine.rs:212-226`).
API handlers commonly build context directly from request headers (`api.rs:341`, `443`, `1053`) without mandatory identity presence checks.
findings.suggestion: Make header-derived contexts unprivileged by default and require explicit trusted construction for system-level contexts; enforce presence/validation of principal headers at API boundary.
